Communities Rising Together

While policy reform moves slowly, Filipino communities aren't waiting. From riverside clean-ups to barangay-led declogging drives, ordinary citizens are taking flood resilience into their own hands.

🧹

Grassroots Clean-Ups

Residents regularly organize creek and canal clean-up drives, clearing the trash and silt that clog drainage during heavy rain.

🏘

Barangay-Led Action

Barangay officials and volunteers mobilize declogging initiatives at the block level — the frontline of flood prevention in Metro Manila.

🤲

Community Resilience

When national infrastructure falls short, it's often the poorest and most flood-exposed communities helping themselves and their neighbors first.

Community in Action

Manila Residents Clean Up Their Own Waterways

🌊 Metro Manila Flood Management

Clearing Creeks, Protecting Neighbors

Millions of Metro Manila residents live in informal settlements built along rivers and creeks — areas that flood fastest and hardest when the rains come. In several of these communities, residents have partnered with local government and civil-society programs to clean up waterways themselves: clearing trash, planting and tending riverbank gardens to prevent informal encroachment, and even building simple fences to keep children safe near the water's edge.

These small, community-driven actions are often the first and most immediate line of defense against flooding — well ahead of any large infrastructure project ever breaking ground.

Source: Mara Warwick, World Bank Country Director. "Philippines: A crucial first step to address Metro Manila's floods." World Bank Blogs, September 2017. Photo credit: Justine E. Letargo / World Bank. Read the full article →
On the Ground

Barangay-Led Declogging Initiatives

Local government units and residents at the barangay level are among the most active — and most overlooked — players in flood prevention. Here are two recent community efforts.

🧹

Barangay 298, Zone 29, District 3 — Declogging Initiative

Community Clean-Up

Residents and volunteers of Barangay 298, Zone 29 in District 3 came together for a community-led declogging drive — clearing canals and waterways of trash and debris to keep drainage flowing and reduce flooding risk in their neighborhood.

🧹

Barangay 673, Zone 73 — Declogging Initiative

Community Clean-Up

Barangay 673, Zone 73 held its own community declogging effort, with residents pitching in to clear silt and blockages from local waterways ahead of the rainy season — a small-scale action with a direct impact on flood risk at the street level.

Solutions & Prevention

What Actually Reduces Flood Risk

Real flood resilience combines long-term structural solutions with everyday precautions households and communities can take right now.

Long-Term Solutions
🌊

Regular Waterway & Drainage Maintenance

Consistent desilting and declogging of creeks, canals, and drainage systems keeps water flowing instead of backing up into streets and homes.

🌳

Nature-Based Solutions

Mangrove restoration, wetlands, rain gardens, and permeable pavements absorb and slow down floodwater instead of letting it pool or rush downstream.

🏙

Smarter Urban Planning

Enforcing zoning laws, protecting waterway easements from informal settlement and construction, and updating drainage master plans as cities grow.

📡

Early Warning Systems

Real-time rain gauges, river-level sensors, and barangay-level alert networks give residents crucial extra minutes — sometimes hours — to evacuate safely.

What You Can Do Now
🧹

Keep Waterways Clear

Never dump trash into canals or esteros. Join or start a community clean-up — it's one of the most effective things ordinary residents can do.

🎒

Prepare a Go-Bag

Keep a ready bag with water, food, flashlight, medicines, important documents, and a power bank — packed before the rainy season starts, not during it.

🗺

Know Your Evacuation Plan

Learn your barangay's evacuation routes and centers in advance, and agree on a family meeting point in case you get separated.

📱

Monitor Official Alerts

Follow PAGASA, your local government unit, and NDRRMC advisories, and evacuate early when instructed — don't wait until water is already inside your home.

💬
